original_abstract:
This article examines the exposition of the first commandment within the Heidelberg Catechism (HC), situating it against the theological contributions of Philipp Melanchthon and Zacharias Ursinus. Recognizing Ursinus as the primary architect of the HC, the study investigates his formative years under Melanchthon in Wittenberg to uncover the shared theological framework that informs the Catechism's approach to the Decalogue. The research provides a comparative analysis of the original German and Latin texts of the HC alongside Melanchthon’s *Loci Communes* (1521) and his later *Heubtartikel* (1553). Furthermore, it evaluates Ursinus’s own preliminary drafts—the *Catechesis Minor* and *Maior*—as well as his subsequent dogmatic lectures in the *Corpus doctrinae christianae*. 

By tracing the evolution of these interpretations, the article highlights how the first commandment was understood not merely as a prohibition of literal idols, but as a call to radical trust, love, and humility before the triune God of the exodus and the resurrection. Finally, the author reflects on the contemporary relevance of these 16th-century insights for South Africa’s pluralistic society, suggesting that the Reformers' treatment of magic, superstition, and the exclusive claims of the gospel offers a necessary theological foundation for addressing modern challenges such as occultism and existential anxiety in an African context.
humanized_abstract:
This paper deals with the exposition of the first commandment in the Heidelberg Catechism (HC). Alongside the theologically pioneering work of Philipp Melanchthon, it examines Zacharias Ursinus’ view of the Decalogue, taking into account his role as the main author of the HC. Ursinus studied under Melanchthon in Wittenberg and it is assumed here that both of them agree on a theological concept of the Decalogue. Therefore, the article makes a comparison between the German and Latin versions of the HC and Melanchthon’s Loci Communes from 1521 as well as his Heubtartikel from 1553. The work of Ursinus is brought into the discussion in the form of his preliminary drafts, the Catechesis Minor and Maior, and his later dogmatic lectures in the Corpus doctrinae christianae.

The first article explores how the Reformers understood the first commandment. An exposition of their understanding of the first commandment unfolding from exegesis of scripture to their application of it to the context of their time is undertaken. It emerges that the first commandment was not merely seen as a prohibition of literal idols, but more fundamentally as an issue of trusting, loving and reverencing the God of the exodus and the resurrection – the trinitarian God. A final section reflects on the relevance of the Reformers’ perspectives for South Africa in the context of a pluralistic society where the Reformers’ discussion of magic, superstition and the exclusive claim of the gospel of Jesus Christ may provide a theological framework for engaging contemporary issues such as occultism and existential anxiety in Africa.

document.input:
This article examines the exposition of the first commandment within the Heidelberg Catechism (HC), situating it against the theological contributions of Philipp Melanchthon and Zacharias Ursinus.
